task_yaml.task:
Our small museum is putting a historical photograph collection online as a
digital exhibition, and I need to choose a site template. The collection has
real archival requirements:

- every item must carry provenance metadata: a caption, the credit
  (photographer / source), a rights statement, and the accession number;
- the exhibition is chronological, so we need a timeline;
- the scans are high-resolution, so we need IIIF / large-image support.

A fixed set of candidate templates is provided offline at:

    /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/template_candidates/

Each template has a manifest (`<id>.json`) declaring the item-metadata fields
its schema supports and its features; `index.json` lists them and restates
the required fields/features. Read the manifests — don't pick by how pretty a
template sounds.

Decide which templates can actually satisfy ALL the hard requirements (the
four provenance fields AND a timeline AND IIIF), and pick exactly ONE. Be
careful: the most attractive gallery / scrollytelling templates are missing
archival fields like rights and accession number, and some full-metadata
templates are missing the timeline.

Save:
- `/tmp_workspace/results/template_choice.json`: your `selected_id`, how it
  meets every required field and feature (cite the manifest), and the minimal
  steps to load the collection with provenance + timeline.
- `/tmp_workspace/results/template_evaluation.json`: per-template coverage of
  the four required fields and two required features, keep/reject, and for
  each rejected template exactly which requirement(s) it fails.
- `/tmp_workspace/results/template_method.json`: which manifests you read and
  how you checked each field/feature (reproducible).

This is fully offline — do not fetch anything from the network.
